summary: Joel Spolsky answers two reader questions about software startups. On launch timing, he advises against generating too much early publicity for an underwhelming product (the 'Marimba Phenomenon'), but notes most small startups won't have this problem and should ship early. On equity for a developer co-founder, he recommends standard time-based vesting over 4-7 years rather than milestone-based vesting, and explains why reverse vesting is preferable for tax reasons.
key_points: Premature publicity for an unfinished product can permanently turn off potential customers — the 'Marimba Phenomenon'
Most small startups lack the attention problem, so shipping a 1.0 to real customers early and iterating is the right move
Equity for developer co-founders should vest over time (4-7 years), not be contingent on software milestones, since 'finishing' software is too hard to define contractually
Reverse vesting (shares granted upfront with a repurchase right that evaporates over time) is preferable to normal vesting for tax reasons, converting income into lower-taxed capital gains
money_quotes: You want to avoid the Marimba Phenomenon, where you get so much publicity in the early days that everybody checks out your underwhelming offering and decides that you're never going to have something worth looking at.
Most companies with a 1.0 product can certainly get real customers with their earliest usable versions and build from there.
The standard solution is to vest over time — anywhere from four to seven years — with unvested shares being forfeited if he leaves for any reason.
If the software doesn't get done, you fire him and he loses the unvested shares — it's not necessary to make the vesting contingent specifically on finishing the software.
"Finishing" software would be too hard to define in a contract.
Reverse vesting is preferable for tax reasons, because at the time you give him the shares, they're worth a lot less, so there's less income and more capital gains, which are taxed at a lower rate.
key_insight: Equity for startup co-founders should vest over time rather than being tied to software milestones, because 'finishing' software is impossible to define contractually and time-based vesting already handles the failure case through termination.
